era_context: Alexander is in the final stages of his Central Asian campaigns, preparing to move into India. Spitamenes' rebellion is being suppressed. In Greece, Macedonian hegemony is secure. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's expansion accelerating. In India, the Nanda Empire dominates the Gangetic plain, unaware of Alexander's imminent arrival.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Qin, Indian, and Persian sources

events.title: Capture and Death of Spitamenes
events.description: Spitamenes, the Sogdian rebel leader, is defeated and killed. According to some accounts, he is betrayed by his own allies among the nomadic tribes and his head is sent to Alexander. The suppression of Spitamenes' rebellion marks the effective end of organized resistance to Macedonian rule in Central Asia. Alexander consolidates control over Bactria and Sogdiana, establishing the eastern frontier of his empire and preparing for the invasion of India.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VIII
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: Spitamenes' death is attested in multiple sources, but dating is uncertain. Most scholars place it in 327 BCE, but some argue for 326 BCE. The exact circumstances of his death (betrayal vs. military defeat) vary across sources.

events.title: Alexander Reaches the Hyphasis River (Sutlej)
events.description: Alexander's army reaches the Hyphasis River (modern Sutlej River) in northwestern India. This represents the furthest extent of Alexander's conquests. The army, exhausted from years of campaigning and facing the prospect of encountering the large armies of the Nanda Empire, refuses to advance further. Alexander is forced to turn back, marking the beginning of his retreat toward Babylon. The Hyphasis represents the practical limit of Macedonian expansion in Asia.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX

events.certainty_note: Alexander's arrival at the Hyphasis and the army's refusal to advance further is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating to 326 BCE is more commonly accepted by modern scholars, but some sources place it in 327 BCE. The event itself is historically certain.

events.title: Cleitus the Black Incident
events.description: Cleitus the Black, one of Alexander's most senior generals and childhood companion, is killed by Alexander in a drunken quarrel. The incident occurs during a banquet and reflects growing tensions between Alexander and his old Macedonian companions over his adoption of Persian customs and increasingly autocratic behavior. Cleitus's death marks a symbolic break between Alexander and the old Macedonian guard. The event is widely interpreted as a turning point in Alexander's reign, demonstrating his willingness to eliminate even his closest associates.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: The death of Cleitus is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating is generally placed in 328 BCE by most modern scholars, though some sources suggest 327 BCE. The event itself is historically certain, though the exact circumstances and Alexander's degree of culpability are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Spitamenes' death is uncertain; most scholars place it in 327 BCE, but some argue for 326 BCE. The Cleitus incident is traditionally dated to 328 BCE by most modern scholars, not 327 BCE, though some sources place it in 327 BCE. The exact year cannot be definitively established. Alexander's arrival at the Hyphasis is often dated to 326 BCE rather than 327 BCE in modern scholarship. The chronology of Alexander's Indian campaign remains contested due to the absence of contemporary sources and inconsistencies in later accounts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 327 BCE remains entirely dependent on later Greek and Roman sources. Arrian, Plutarch, and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, all writing centuries after the events. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. The chronology of events in Central Asia and the transition to India is notoriously difficult to establish, and modern scholars continue to debate the exact sequence and dating. Archaeological evidence from Central Asia and northwestern India for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible. Indian and Persian perspectives are entirely absent from surviving sources. The Cleitus incident, while famous, may have been embellished or reinterpreted by later sources to illustrate Alexander's character.

graph_edges.note: Suppression of Sogdiana enables Alexander's invasion of India
Arrival at Hyphasis leads to Battle of Hydaspes and retreat
Cleitus's death continues pattern of Alexander eliminating rivals
